book.title: Kitchen Confidential
book.author: Anthony Bourdain
book.notes: Read this for the third time after opening Honeycomb. Different every time. The part about mise en place hit harder than usual.

book.title: The Art of Fermentation
book.author: Sandor Katz
book.notes: Dense, rewarding. Changed how I think about Ruth at different temperatures. Took notes in the margin.

book.title: The E-Myth Revisited
book.author: Michael Gerber
book.notes: Conceptually useful. Overwritten. The point is systems, not heroics. I got it by chapter 4.

book.title: Plenty
book.author: Yotam Ottolenghi
book.notes: More recipe book than memoir but beautiful. Pushed me and Nate toward more roasted vegetable cooking at home.

book.title: Bread Science
book.author: Emily Buehler
book.notes: Best technical bread book I have read. Explains the why behind hydration, fermentation, and crust. Bought a second copy for the bakery.

book.title: The Yellow House
book.author: Sarah M. Broom
book.notes: Not food or business, Tamara lent it to me. About New Orleans and family. Slow burn, worth it.

book.title: Setting the Table
book.author: Danny Meyer
book.notes: Best hospitality book I have read. His model for staff retention is what I think about whenever the Kenji question comes up.

book.title: Salt Fat Acid Heat
book.author: Samin Nosrat
book.notes: Finally read it. Every sentence earns its place. The acid chapter changed how I build flavor in GF bakes.

book.title: Unreasonable Hospitality
book.author: Will Guidara
book.notes: Shorter and easier than Meyer but same DNA. Good for reminders.

book.title: The Lost Kitchen
book.author: Erin French
book.notes: Read in a weekend. Her chapter on the burnout year felt personal. Nate noticed I was quiet after that one.

book.title: An Everlasting Meal
book.author: Tamar Adler
book.notes: Slow read, in the best way. Shifted how we approach weeknight cooking with Nate, less planning, more instinct.

book.title: Good to Great
book.author: Jim Collins
book.notes: Useful data and framing. Feels dated. The Flywheel concept is the one thing I carry.

book.title: Tender at the Bone
book.author: Ruth Reichl
book.notes: Named my starter Ruth after re-reading this. Still the best food coming-of-age book.

book.title: Profit First
book.author: Mike Michalowicz
book.notes: Useful for forcing cash allocation discipline. Applied the envelope logic to our savings split with Nate.

book.title: Blood, Bones, and Butter
book.author: Gabrielle Hamilton
book.notes: Best food memoir I have read since Bourdain. Honest, difficult, precise.

book.title: Small Business Big Life
book.author: Louis Barajas
book.notes: Did not land for me. Finished it out of stubbornness.

book.title: Cooked
book.author: Michael Pollan
book.notes: The bread chapter is mandatory reading. The fire chapter surprised me. A bit long overall.

book.title: The Glass Hotel
book.author: Emily St. John Mandel
book.notes: Tamara's pick for Thursday. Nate read it too and we talked about it for a week.

book.title: This Is Not a T-Shirt
book.author: Bobby Hundreds
book.notes: Brand-building from an independent perspective. Made me think about Honeycomb's visual identity more seriously.

book.title: What I Talk About When I Talk About Running
book.author: Haruki Murakami
book.notes: Read during holiday crunch in 20-minute intervals before bed. Really about discipline.

book.title: Big Magic
book.author: Elizabeth Gilbert
book.notes: Fine. Inspirational but not specific enough to stick. Gift from Deb.

book.title: Tartine Bread
book.author: Chad Robertson
book.notes: Re-read, not for the first time. Benchmark text. The section on country dough is still the best thing written about sourdough.

book.title: The Checklist Manifesto
book.author: Atul Gawande
book.notes: Strong argument for checklists. Applied immediately, wrote a proper GF prep area protocol after reading this.

book.title: Mastering Bread
book.author: Marc Vetri
book.notes: Technical and deep. Made me rethink einkorn hydration. Slow to read but worth it.

book.title: Four Thousand Weeks
book.author: Oliver Burkeman
book.notes: Tamara gave me this during a burnout stretch. The point, you cannot do everything. Reread page 40 twice.

book.title: Zingerman's Guide to Good Eating
book.author: Ari Weinzweig
book.notes: Useful for thinking about sourcing and storytelling. Some GF-relevant notes in the back.

book.title: The Hard Thing About Hard Things
book.author: Ben Horowitz
book.notes: Startup perspective, not small business. Some applicable leadership principles. A bit macho for my taste.

book.title: Bread and Wine
book.author: Shauna Niequist
book.notes: Not usually my genre but Gail gave it to me. The chapter on dietary restriction was unexpectedly moving.

book.title: Good Strategy, Bad Strategy
book.author: Richard Rumelt
book.notes: Best strategy book in three years. Cleaned up my thinking about the third baker decision and Broad Street Grounds.

book.title: Salt in the Margins
book.author: Beth Lindgren
book.notes: Currently reading before bed. About the Gulf Coast fishing industry and the people who cook what they catch. Very good so far.
